explanation: The phrase can be interpreted as: 'Whenever prop_0 and prop_2 both hold, prop_1 should also hold.' In other words, for all times, if both prop_0 and prop_2 are true, then prop_1 must be true as well.

The phrase suggests that whenever 'prop_0' holds, it should be accompanied by 'prop_1', and this requirement is associated with 'prop_2'. Typically, 'for the prop_2' attaches the scenario within which the requirement holds; that is, while 'prop_2' holds. Therefore, globally, whenever 'prop_2' holds, 'prop_0 -> prop_1' should also hold.

The statement requires that whenever prop_0 is true, then prop_1 must hold during the time that prop_2 holds—i.e., while prop_2 remains true. This expresses that for every time prop_0 happens, prop_1 needs to be satisfied as long as prop_2 holds.

The specification in natural language can be interpreted as: Whenever 'prop_0' occurs, 'prop_1' must hold throughout the duration of 'prop_2'. This is expressed by stating that globally, if 'prop_0' occurs, then 'prop_1' must hold as long as 'prop_2' holds (possibly starting when 'prop_0' does). This is represented with the Release operator as 'prop_2 R prop_1', so that 'prop_1' must hold at least as long as 'prop_2' does. The LTL formula below represents that globally, if 'prop_0' is true, then 'prop_2 R prop_1' holds.

The sentence suggests that whenever 'prop_0' occurs, 'prop_1' must hold during the time 'prop_2' is true (or while 'prop_2' lasts). That is, for any instance of 'prop_0', as long as 'prop_2' holds, 'prop_1' must hold as well. This is captured by saying that globally, if 'prop_0' occurs, then ('prop_2' releases 'prop_1'), which means 'prop_1' must hold at least as long as 'prop_2' does (if 'prop_2' ends, the obligation for 'prop_1' ends).

The phrase can be interpreted as: When 'prop_0' occurs, 'prop_1' holds concurrent with 'prop_2'. In LTL, ensuring that whenever 'prop_0' happens, during the time 'prop_2' holds, 'prop_1' must also hold. A simple, safe formulation is to say: globally, if 'prop_0' and 'prop_2' hold, then 'prop_1' holds as well.

Interpreting the sentence, it suggests that when 'prop_0' occurs, then 'prop_1' must hold during the period in which 'prop_2' holds. This is typically expressed such that whenever 'prop_0' becomes true, it is required that 'prop_1' holds at least as long as 'prop_2' holds (i.e., as long as 'prop_2' is true following the 'prop_0' event). In LTL, this can be modeled with a release operator: when 'prop_0' holds, then 'prop_2' releases 'prop_1' – so 'prop_1' must continue to hold until 'prop_2' is false.

The phrase suggests that whenever 'prop_0' holds, then 'prop_1' should hold as long as 'prop_2' holds. This is best expressed as: globally, if 'prop_0' holds, then ('prop_2' releases 'prop_1'). The release operator captures that 'prop_1' must remain true at least until 'prop_2' becomes false (and possibly beyond).

The phrase 'A prop_0 shall prop_1 for the prop_2' suggests that whenever prop_0 occurs, prop_1 must hold during the duration of prop_2. We express this in LTL as: whenever prop_0 occurs, prop_1 holds at least as long as prop_2 holds. This is done using the releases (V) operator: (prop_2 V prop_1) means that either prop_1 holds until prop_2 becomes true, or prop_1 holds globally (if prop_2 never happens). So, whenever prop_0 holds, (prop_2 V prop_1) should hold.

The phrase 'A prop_0 shall prop_1.' typically means that whenever prop_0 occurs, prop_1 must also occur at the same time. In LTL, this is captured by the safety property 'Globally, if prop_0 is true, then prop_1 is also true.'
